narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on academic records, test scores, family background, and other details from past students. The model estimated a 41.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which crossed the threshold for rejection, as schools aim to admit those with stronger predicted odds of success to maintain high pass rates. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4 (below the average of 5.71), a Year 3 decile of 4 (below the average of 5.53), an undergraduate GPA of 2.90 (under the typical 3.21), an LSAT score of 30 (well below the average 36.70), middle family income, and plans for full-time enrollment, which is the norm for 92.7% of students. Despite these factors, the three most influential elements in the model's decision all worked in your favor, pushing the prediction toward passing rather than failing—yet the overall risk remained too high. The strongest positive influence came from your middle family income bracket, which the model sees as a supportive factor linked to better bar outcomes compared to lower incomes. Next, your Year 3 decile of 4 provided a moderate boost, suggesting your later academic performance offered some reassurance of potential improvement. Your choice of full-time study rounded out the top influences, as it aligns with the majority of successful students and signals commitment without divided attention. These elements helped offset concerns from your lower LSAT, GPA, and earlier deciles, but not enough to tip the scales below the failure threshold. In comparison to typical students who get approved, your test scores and grades sit notably lower, which amplified the perceived risk despite the helpful aspects of your background. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a Black man pursuing law—many overcome similar starts through targeted preparation. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, strengthening your academic record with additional coursework or certifications, and reapplying to schools with holistic reviews that weigh personal stories like yours. Building bar-specific study habits now could also demonstrate readiness in future applications, turning this setback into a stepping stone.
